Hi,

I have some concerns on the evaluations used throughout this code repository.

PPO/TRPO

The evaluation uses the same trajectories as for training. However, you have a horizon which could be a partial trajectory. However, if you do evaluation on these partial trajectories, you may be skewing your results for the worse. For example, say we have a batch size of 2048 (as is the default with mujoco). And we have two trajectories of 1000 timesteps and then a partial trajectory of 48 timesteps, it looks like our average returns will be much lower than they actually are since we're using the partial trajectory in our evaluation.

Offending line of code: https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/master/baselines/pposgd/pposgd_simple.py#L36

DDPG:

First, the algorithm seems a bit off, you collect 100 time steps, then train for 40 timesteps and then evaluate for another 100 and then loop this 20 times. This has the feel of n-step returns without making use of n-step returns. As I understand it originally DDPG should take a small number of steps (ideally one) and use the replay buffer. Second, you're actually evaluating across at least 10 different policies it seems for one trajectory.

https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/master/baselines/ddpg/training.py#L122-L139

This seems very off. I think the proper thing would be to move the evaluation loop outside of the training loop and do N proper full trajectories with a single policy per epoch. And again, here you may not be evaluating the full trajectory.

Maybe I'm misunderstanding the codebase due to the MPI details, but it seems like overall the evaluations done are not proper and can either beneficially or detrimentally change results (even with the 100 average return running mean).

issue_title: Supervised learning - different vector file outputs for binary vs text pretrained vectors
issue_body:
During supervised learning, when the pretrained vectors are provided in binary format, the model vectors ( .vec file) only include words seen in the training data.

When using text format pretrained vectors, the model vectors includes all the original words, but the vectors for words not seen in training data are all set to zeros.

In BaseBackend, send two signals around outbound messages:

1. pre-send: after the payload is constructed, but before the send.
  Gives receivers a chance to cancel the send (e.g., to implement
  blacklists) or otherwise muck with the payload, or to log a
  sent-message model (e.g., for an email tracking dashboard)

2. post-send: with the normalized response (including message id).
  Good place to (e.g.,) update your sent-message model with the
  message id.

kubectl respects KUBECONFIG for its configuration file location.  In addition to the obvious, it also supports *multiple* configuration files in this variable and merges them.

See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/organize-cluster-access-kubeconfig/#the-kubeconfig-environment-variable for details

It would be great to have a simple API for creating a `KubeConfig` obeying this convention.  For example:

    KubeConfig.from_kubeconfig_env(os.environ['KUBECONFIG'])

which might be a building block of a future API like:

    KubeConfig.from_convention(os.environ)

(which would look for `KUBECONFIG` in the given environ dict and then fall back to `$HOME/.kube/config` if the var is not present)

issue_body:
As outlined at https://github.com/pimutils/vdirsyncer/issues/574#issuecomment-284148130, vdirsyncer's default useragent does not conform to the relevant RFCs. We should include the version number like this:

    vdirsyncer/0.15

The new HTTP RFC specifies the syntax [here](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7231#section-5.5.3)

issue_title: Stop telling devs to run certbot_test against staging
issue_body:
@sydneyli brought to my attention that the way we're telling developers to use `certbot_test` in https://certbot.eff.org/docs/contributing.html#running-a-local-copy-of-the-client does not work. You cannot use `certbot_test` against the staging server because `certbot_test` uses alternate ports for challenges which isn't supported in staging.

In my opinion, we should remove the recommendation of using `certbot_test` entirely and tell people to run the client as root with the staging flag.
`--renew--hook` should probably be supplanted by `--deploy-hook`, which should run when you first make a cert as well as when you renew one.

Brainstorming about verbs, we decided to improve the top level output of `cerbot -h`:

```
certbot --help

  certbot [SUBCOMMAND] [options] [-d domain] [-d domain] ...

Certbot can obtain and install HTTPS/TLS/SSL certificates.  By default,
it will attempt to use a webserver both for obtaining and installing the
cert. The most common SUBCOMMANDS and flags are:
    
obtain, install, and renew certificates:
  (default) run       Obtain & install a cert in your current webserver
  certonly             Obtain or renew a cert, but do not install it
  renew                Renew all previously obtained certs that are near expiry

--apache          Use the Apache plugin for authentication & installation
--standalone      Run a standalone webserver for authentication
--nginx           Use the Nginx plugin for authentication & installation
--webroot         Place files in a server's webroot folder for authentication
--manual          Obtain certs interactively, or using shell script hoooks
 -n               Run non-interactively

manage certificates:    
  certificates         Display information about certs configured with Certbot
  manage             Interactively manage your certificates
  rename              Update a certificate's name 
  revoke               Revoke a previously obtained certificate
    
manage your account with Let's Encrypt:
  register           Create an account

issue_title: Ensure that `smoketest` uses an unused port for ethereum rpc
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

The current `smoketest` implementation has a stub implementation `get_free_port` that always returns the first port of the pre setup port range. The idea of `get_free_port` is to chose a free port during the smoketest.

## Solution

Either change the implementation of `smoketest.py::get_free_port` to actually check for a free port, or use a fixed port and add a user friendly error condition for a port clash.

issue_title: Use md5sum for large input parameters / variables
issue_body:
For parameters, with or without `parameter:` statement, we currently save them as is. But suppose I have this setup: 

```
[global]
x_mat = simulated_large_matrix()
```

and `x_mat` will be passed through every step of the workflow. As a result signature for each step can be large. For example I've got 4759 task files in `~/.sos/tasks`. The total size is 258M. This is just a result of about 9 hours of computation. I think we should worry about task file size and use md5sum for large input variables.

issue_title: ParseError message does not provide enough insight
issue_body:
### Actual behavior

The message text of `zhmcclient.ParseError` exceptions is pretty thin and does not provide sufficient context. for example:

```
ParseError: Parse error in returned JSON: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)
```

### Expected behavior

In this example, the response content seems to be empty, but the particular function in the zhmcclient code that raises this `ParseError` should be called only for status codes that indicate non-empty content. Therefore, interesting additional information would be the HTTP method and URL, and the HTTP status code.

### Execution environment

* zhmcclient version: 2.13.0
* Operating system (type+version): RHEL 7.3
* HMC version: 2.13.1
* CPC version: 2.13.1

I tried using SOCIAL_AUTH_SAML_EXTRA_DATA to get the extra attributes into UserSocialAuth.extra_data but I don't believe this has been implemented yet. I suppose it could be done by subclassing SAMLAuth and overriding _check_entitlements() but I think that would be an abuse of what that method is for. Probably the right fix is to implement a SAMLAuth().extra_data(). I'll take a stab at it but wanted to submit this issue to track it. Please someone correct me if I'm wrong.

issue_title: Same function assigned different IDs on driver and worker
issue_body:
### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**: Linux Ubuntu 17.10
- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**: Binary
- **Ray version**: 0.40
- **Python version**: Python 3.6.5
- **Exact command to reproduce**:

### Describe the problem

ray/worker.py:compute_function_id checks whether __main__ has the __file__ attribute to decide whether to include the source code in the function hash. When starting a Python executable using ```python -m mypkg.executable```, dependencies in mypkg can be imported before __main__ is loaded, resulting in this check failing (and source code being excluded) in the driver. When it comes to running the code, however, __main__ exists. This can lead to function IDs differing between the driver and worker in nested parallelism settings.

The error I get is similar to that of #1446 so it may have a common cause. After diagnosing this I think I've become convinced that it is best for executables to be in the top-level and not nested in the package structure; I'm posting this mostly as a warning to others as it is far from obvious what caused this error.

I think we can make this more robust by using a try-except statement rather than guessing when the source code is present; I'll submit a PR.

### Source code / logs
This bug is only triggered with a particular package structure, so this needs a few files. First, create a directory foo containing:

```python3
# __init__.py
from foo import bar
```

```python3
# main.py
import ray                                                                                                                                                                                            

from foo import bar

if __name__ == '__main__':
    ray.init(redirect_worker_output=True)
    bar.run()
```

import ray

def f(x):

def g(x):

@ray.remote
def h(x):

def run():

```

issue_title: Cannot use custom template tags / filters
issue_body:
Hi,

First, let me thank you for this great project, it appears to bring really useful functionality to Django!

I'm facing a problem with the HTML Layout object, and couldn't find any existing issue for it. When using my own custom template filter, I get an _invalid filter_ error when using something like:
`HTML('{{ some_value|my_template_filter }}')`
To have it work, I must write:
`HTML('{% load my_tag_module %}{{ some_value|my_template_filter }}')`

My filter works well, and I correctly added the {% load my_tag_module %} at the beginning of my template file. Moreover, using it outside the crispy form works well. Obviously, the HTML object doesn't seem to inherit the loaded template modules.

Maybe I've missed something ? Anyway a simplistic workaround would be to define a method that will prepend the module loading before the HTML code:

```
def html_with_custom_tags(tag_lib, html_code, *args, **kwargs):
    return HTML("{%% load %s %%}%s" % (tag_lib, html_code), *args, **kwargs)
...
# call the user-defined method instead of HTML object
html_with_custom_tags('my_tag_module', '{{ some_value|my_template_filter }}')
```

I agree, that's not the greatest solution ever, just a "quick-and-dirty" patch... :-)

I don't know if this should be considered an issue, but you may want to add something in the documentation about it?

Cheers,

Pascal.

issue_title: docker network support for spawning lambda images in local stack
issue_body:
Case scenario:
Given Docker Compose stack, where one of the lambdas spawned needs to communicate to a mongo db image in the same stack. While all other images may be sharing networks, the newly spawned docker image for the lambda is not connected to the network.

Thus the connection and lambda fails.

No other option or configuration provided to connect the lambda image to main network
Also, add hdf5 as possible input to pipeline.

Currently, you have to provide an area of interest for `sentinelsat`. But I often know exactly which tile(s) I am interested in. It would be awesome to have a function to download specific tiles, like [`sentinelhub`](https://github.com/sinergise/sentinelhub) has (but without the awkward `tile, datestr` tuples ;-).

As far as I can see, DHuS unfortunately does not have a high-level function to search by tile (see their [Full Text Search](https://scihub.copernicus.eu/userguide/3FullTextSearch) docs).

But you can search for the tile ID in the file name, like `filename:*_TDDABC_*` which however will only work for the [post-2016 single-tile product format](https://earth.esa.int/web/sentinel/user-guides/sentinel-2-msi/naming-convention).

NB: For some reason, combining several `filename` patterns with `OR` only returns the last (e.g. `filename:*_T32UPF_* OR filename:*_T32UMF_*`) and `filename:(*_T33UPF_* OR *_T32UMF_*)` does not work at all.

What do you think, should we add a high-level function to the CLI / API to search by tile? It could create the `filename` search pattern and perhaps run several queries when several tiles are provided - unless there is a better way with DHuS that I did not see...
Remove caching on bel pipeline nanopub url - it will not select new version of nanopub for BEL pipeline.

issue_title: `set_clusters` should use string type for cluster labels for hdf5 dump
issue_body:
Hi,
when setting clusters from an external data frame (eg., read Seurat output using pandas with `vlm.set_clusters(my_annotation.loc[vlm.ca['CellID'],'cell.type'])`), the dtype of vlm.cluster_labels is `'O'`, which cannot be dumped into hdf5 (`TypeError: Object dtype dtype('O') has no native HDF5 equivalent`).
using `vlm.cluster_labels=vlm.cluster_labels.astype(np.string_)` fixes this problem.

great package by the way!

issue_title: Explore decorator-based API for solids to eliminate boilerplate
N3 should write integers without ^^<type> syntax
issue_body:
coryd...@gmail.com, 2009-07-12T06:44:54.000Z 
What steps will reproduce the problem?
1. Load the following graph, then serialize it:

:a :b 1.


What is the expected output? What do you see instead?

Expected:  :a :b 1.
Actual output: :a :b "1"^^.


The attached 6-line patch fixes this.  I will commit this to 2.4.x branch
if nobody has any objections.  Please let me know.

Comment 1 by coryd...@gmail.com
Could someone please comment?  I'm not sure whether the change I'm proposing is
backward-compatible.  Also, I don't know how to run the unit tests to make sure I
didn't break anything. :-)


Comment 2 by lindstr...@gmail.com
This behaviour is the default now in trunk (r1708).

(The difference between xsd:decimal, xsd:float and xsd:double may be problematic for plain literal serializations 
though, so that feature may need some verification before plain literals are positively fixed. (See also the boolean 
in n3 comments in term.py#Literal (should be correlated with the n3 parser)..))


Comment 3 by eik...@gmail.com



Comment 4 by gromgull
as lindstream said, fixed in r1708. 
I verified now.


Comment 5 by lindstr...@gmail.com
For the record, as of r1781 the plain form of booleans (true/false) is also supported 
(since the new N3 parser also handles that).
